methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $500M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 2 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 6 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Q-CTRL, a Series B quantum computing software company founded by Michael Biercuk, was most recently valued at $500M post-money after raising $132M to date. The quantum computing sector contains 11 identifiable comparables (3 public, 8 private), including peers such as IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion, though most operate across different technology stacks and commercial models.

A single triangulation method proved applicable to this valuation. Stage-progression analysis, anchored to the $500M Series B post-money valuation, applied a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B to subsequent-stage advancement, yielding $950M. This method directly reflects market-observed funding step-ups within the sector and accounts for incremental value creation between financing rounds.

Peer-median analysis was inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables meeting minimum sample thresholds (fewer than 3 datapoints in narrow filters). The broader quantum computing universe proved too heterogeneous in commercial model and maturity to produce reliable median positioning without introducing noise.

The valuation analysis supports a range of $808M (low) to $1.1B (high), with a median of $950M. This represents a 1.6x to 2.2x multiple on the current $500M valuation. Key uncertainties include the absence of strong same-stage peer liquidity events to validate step-up assumptions and divergence in quantum computing business models limiting comparability. Downside risk centers on funding market conditions and technical progress validation across the sector.
